---
title: "Configuring schemes"
description: "Auto-generate and customize Xcode schemes for different environments and configurations"
---

XcodeGen automatically generates one scheme per target. For more control — multiple environment variants, specific test targets, custom actions, or reusable templates — you can define schemes explicitly in `project.yml`.

## Auto-generated schemes

By default, every target gets a scheme with the target name. The scheme uses the first debug-type configuration for the run, test, and analyze actions, and the first release-type configuration for profile and archive.

## Target Scheme

The `scheme` property on a target is a shorthand for generating environment-variant schemes and linking test targets without defining a full top-level scheme. This is the most common way to configure schemes.

```yaml
configs:
  Test Debug: debug
  Staging Debug: debug
  Production Debug: debug
  Test Release: release
  Staging Release: release
  Production Release: release

targets:
  MyApp:
    scheme:
      testTargets:
        - MyUnitTests
      configVariants:
        - Test
        - Staging
        - Production
      gatherCoverageData: true
      coverageTargets:
        - MyApp
      commandLineArguments:
        "-MyEnabledArg": true
        "-MyDisabledArg": false
      environmentVariables:
        MY_ENV_VAR: VALUE
  MyUnitTests:
    sources: Tests
```

The `configVariants` list generates one scheme per entry. Each scheme matches configurations containing that variant name (case-insensitive substring match), using debug configs for run/test/analyze and release configs for profile/archive. The above example produces:

- `MyApp Test`
- `MyApp Staging`
- `MyApp Production`

## Full Scheme Definition

Use the top-level `schemes` key when you need full control over every action in a scheme.

```yaml
schemes:
  Production:
    build:
      targets:
        MyTarget1: all
        MyTarget2: [run, archive]
      parallelizeBuild: true
      buildImplicitDependencies: true
    run:
      config: prod-debug
      commandLineArguments:
        "-MyEnabledArg": true
        "-MyDisabledArg": false
      environmentVariables:
        RUN_ENV_VAR: VALUE
    test:
      config: prod-debug
      gatherCoverageData: true
      coverageTargets:
        - MyTarget1
      targets:
        - Tester1
        - name: Tester2
          parallelizable: true
          randomExecutionOrder: true
          skippedTests: [Test/testExample()]
      commandLineArguments:
        "-MyEnabledArg": true
      environmentVariables:
        - variable: TEST_ENV_VAR
          value: VALUE
          isEnabled: false
    profile:
      config: prod-release
    analyze:
      config: prod-debug
    archive:
      config: prod-release
      customArchiveName: MyApp
      revealArchiveInOrganizer: false
```

### Build targets

The `build.targets` map accepts a target name as the key and one of the following as the value:

- `all` — enable for all build types
- `none` — disable for all build types
- An array of build types: `run`, `test`, `profile`, `analyze`, `archive`

### Common action options

The `run`, `test`, `profile`, `analyze`, and `archive` actions all share these properties:

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| `config` | The build configuration to use |
| `preActions` | Scripts to run before the action |
| `postActions` | Scripts to run after the action |
| `commandLineArguments` | Map of argument to enabled state (`run`, `test`, `profile` only) |
| `environmentVariables` | Environment variables (`run`, `test`, `profile` only) |

### Pre/Post actions

Execution actions run shell scripts before or after a build action:

```yaml
schemes:
  MyApp:
    build:
      targets:
        MyApp: all
      preActions:
        - script: echo "Starting build"
          name: Log Build Start
          settingsTarget: MyApp
      postActions:
        - script: |
            echo "Build finished"
            notify_slack.sh
          name: Notify Slack
```

## Scheme Templates

Use `schemeTemplates` to extract common scheme settings and reuse them across multiple schemes. Any `${scheme_name}` placeholder in a template is replaced with the referencing scheme's name.

```yaml
schemes:
  MyModule:
    templates:
      - FeatureModuleScheme
    templateAttributes:
      testTargetName: MyModuleTests

schemeTemplates:
  FeatureModuleScheme:
    templates:
      - TestScheme
    build:
      targets:
        ${scheme_name}: build

  TestScheme:
    test:
      gatherCoverageData: true
      targets:
        - name: ${testTargetName}
          parallelizable: true
          randomExecutionOrder: true
```

This produces a `MyModule` scheme that builds `MyModule` and runs tests against `MyModuleTests`. Scheme templates are especially useful in modular apps where every module follows the same structure.

## Test Plans

Test plans are not generated by XcodeGen and must be created in Xcode first. Once created, reference them by path:

```yaml
schemes:
  MyApp:
    test:
      testPlans:
        - path: MyApp.xctestplan
          defaultPlan: true
```

<Note>
If test plan paths don't exist at generation time, XcodeGen will error. You can suppress this with `options.disabledValidations: [missingTestPlans]` while the plans are being set up.
</Note>
